title: Telecom quantum memory over one microsecond in nanophotonic lithium niobate
authors: Priyash Barya, Daren Chen, Ashwith Prabhu, Laura Heller, Edmond Chow, Hansol Kim, Joshua Akin, Vasileios Niaouris, Jiefei Zhang, Alan M. Dibos, Pengjie Wang, Elizabeth A. Goldschmidt
abstract: Nanophotonic quantum memory is a vital component for scalable quantum information processing in quantum computing, networking, and sensing. Here we store single-photon-level telecom-band optical pulses for more than 1 microsecond using an atomic frequency comb in erbium-doped thin-film lithium niobate, far exceeding what is practically achievable by propagation in even the best nanophotonic devices because of propagation losses. We verify the quantum nature of this storage by demonstrating phase coherence and sub-single-photon noise upon retrieval. We also show the flexibility of our platform by storing up to 20 temporal modes and demonstrating an acceptance bandwidth up to 2.2 GHz. These results establish erbium-doped thin-film lithium niobate as a practical platform for on-chip quantum memory at telecom wavelengths, a key missing element for photonic quantum computing and quantum networking.
summary: The study demonstrates a quantum memory in erbium-doped thin-film lithium niobate capable of storing telecom-band photons for over one microsecond. It achieves this by using the atomic frequency comb protocol to bypass the propagation loss limits of standard nanophotonics. The platform also shows high-bandwidth and multimode storage capabilities, making it a promising candidate for integrated quantum networks.
main_problem: The need for scalable, integrated quantum memory at telecom wavelengths that can overcome the short-duration storage limits imposed by propagation losses in nanophotonic devices.
main_result: Demonstrated telecom-band optical pulse storage for over 1 microsecond in an erbium-doped thin-film lithium niobate platform, with proven phase coherence and broadband/multimode capabilities.
method: The researchers used the Atomic Frequency Comb (AFC) protocol in erbium-doped thin-film lithium niobate (TFLN) waveguides, employing spectral hole burning and measuring retrieval efficiency, coherence, and interference visibility.
model_or_system: An experimental platform consisting of erbium-doped thin-film lithium niobate (Er:TFLN) nanophotonic ridge waveguides operated in a dilution refrigerator at 30 mK under a 1.5 T magnetic field.
key_observables: Storage time, retrieval efficiency, optical coherence time (T2), interference visibility, optical depth (OD), and noise figure.
important_parameters: Storage times up to 1.2 microseconds, optical coherence time of 90 microseconds, optical depth of 1.3, and a demonstrated bandwidth of 2.2 GHz.
main_assumptions: The extraction of coherence time assumes a specific fitting model for photon echo intensity to account for spectral diffusion; efficiency is limited by low optical depth and comb finesse.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the device schematic; Figure 2 demonstrates coherent storage via interference fringes; Figure 3 shows multimode storage capability and broadband performance.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of nanophotonic storage limits, describes the Er:TFLN platform and AFC protocol, presents experimental results for storage time and efficiency, demonstrates quantum coherence and multimode/broadband capabilities, and concludes with future scaling pathways.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and open quantum systems as it demonstrates long-lived coherent storage of telecom photons in a scalable, integrated nanophotonic architecture, providing a platform for studying light-matter interaction and quantum networking.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Quantum Memory on TFLN. (a) Schematic of our Er-doped TFLN waveguides for quantum memory. (b) The inset shows a chip-scale device containing numerous repeating functional waveguides in the inset, highlighting its scalability. (c) AFC storage of weak coherent pulses (¯n = 1) for ten different storage times from 300 ns to 1.2 µs spaced at 100 ns (different colors). The undelayed leakage light is shown for reference at time zero (with 5x higher scale). Inset: Atomic frequency comb for 300 ns storage time with finesse F ≈2.5 measured via transmission of weak probe light.

FIG. 2. Coherent Storage. Retrieved photons in the cen- tral time bin as a function of phase between the early and late components of the stored time-bin encoded pulse. Error bars represent Poissonian statistical uncertainties. Visibility is ex- tracted from the fitted sinusoidal function (red line). Inset shows the output pulses at the points of maximum construc- tive (orange) and destructive (blue) interference.

FIG. 3. Multimode and Broadband Storage. (a) Storage of 20 temporal modes for 1 µs. Stored pulses have a FWHM = 8.7 ns and ¯n = 1, same as those in Figure 1c. (b) Storage of a 200-ps input pulse (inset shows temporal shape with ¯n = 1) with a bandwidth of 2.2 GHz for different storage times. The undelayed leakage light is shown for reference at time zero.
